original_abstract: The debate surrounding GPT-3.5 has intensified within the academic community since the launch of ChatGPT. While some scholars highlight its potential to streamline and enhance academic writing, others raise serious ethical concerns regarding plagiarism, the dissemination of misinformation, and the environmental costs of large-scale AI models. Despite calls for more transparent usage and "smart" integration strategies on social media, there is a significant gap in the literature regarding concrete workflows and efficacy in research contexts. To address this, I conducted a personal auto-ethnographic experiment, utilizing OpenAI’s text-davinci-003 model to draft this article. Through this process, I identified and evaluated five distinct interaction methods: Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and Polisher. I reflect on the effectiveness of each approach and discuss their broader implications for writing ethics and authorial agency. To ensure full transparency, I have provided a comprehensive supplementary document that includes the exact prompts used, the original AI outputs, and the subsequent human edits, quantified by percentage difference.
humanized_abstract: I wrote this post with the aid of a text generator and then commented on the experience. Last month I wrote a bit about GPT-3.5. Since then ChatGPT has appeared in the world of human English and the discussion about GPT-3.5 has turned to its potential to assist in academic writing and the attendant concerns over possible plagiarism, misinformation and the carbon footprint of the massive language model behind the application. OpenAI’s other application, DALL-E, has just appeared with a better range of choices for its “smart” uses on social media, but I found that there is surprisingly little information available on practical methods and evidence of its use in research. In my attempt to get a feel for how the new text generator functions I decided to carry out a simple experiment and wrote this post using the OpenAI application that is hosted using the text-davinci-003 model. I investigated how different modes of interaction work with the model and chose to test five different functionalities, labelled as respectively Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy and Polisher, that offer differing levels of assistance to an author. My intention is to describe and evaluate their effectiveness as tools in writing research and to ponder how these tools may affect writing ethics and authors’ roles. This post therefore aims to lay bare in more detail the underlying mechanisms of these applications that are only barely accessible at present, and their potential for disrupting present writing practices. In order to trace in full detail all of the modifications that the AI carries out in relation to the input given by a user I have put together a reference document listing the inputs for each mode of interaction, the corresponding results provided by the application and the human corrections, including their absolute values and the percentage difference in relation to the initial input for each modified sentence.
